next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The sixth-floor graduate office smelled of wet wool and the hot-dust cough the radiator gave off every time it woke. Outside the long window the lake looked skinned over with dirty pewter. June stood at the sill in her work boots, chewing nicotine gum hard enough that the muscle in her jaw kept ticking. Omar had taken off his coat and folded it over the back of the chair with the same care he used on shirts that cost more than any of them admitted. Leela sat at her desk with the grievance PDF open again, as if another pass through the thing might generate the evidence she had refused to include.

It was still the weakened version. No server logs. No clean line from Mohit's private clones to Ruderal. Their best chance had gone out in a file that smelled, even to her, of fear.

Ben texted, `Are you all upstairs? Need 5 min. Actually important.`

June looked over when Leela read it aloud. "He writes like a hostage trying to sound casual."

"Maybe he finally found a spine," Omar said.

Nobody believed that, but nobody said not to let him up.

Ben came in carrying cold with him. He had on one of his expensive winter jackets, navy and too new-looking for Keating, and the lift-ticket band from some January ski weekend was still cinched uselessly around the top loop of his backpack. He never took those little proofs of another life off soon enough. His cheeks were red from the wind. He shut the door with more care than the moment deserved and stayed with his hand on the knob a second too long, as if he might still choose a different room.

"What happened?" Leela asked...
open_hooks_to_carry: Build suspense through document assembly, memory comparison, and missing files rather than through outward action.
Make the group's temporary collaboration feel painstaking and almost intimate.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...a wild second she imagined Mohit somewhere across campus in a donor meeting, feeling his pocket vibrate and knowing before he looked exactly what had happened.

Ben was still standing by the desk. "Could you send the email now?" he asked. "Just so it's concrete."

Leela turned and stared at him. He flushed again, but he did not back down. Of course he didn't. He had learned from the same teacher she had.

"You really want this timestamped in the next thirty seconds," she said.

"I want to know I'm not the only idiot in the room."

That, at least, was honest.

She sat at Mohit's desk because there was nowhere else to do it and opened her own email on the edge of the iMac screen. Her wrist brace brushed the keyboard. The office smelled more strongly of cedar now, or maybe she had stopped noticing everything else. She typed Ben's address. For one second she thought of June, of the paper that already had too much rot in it, of how easily authorship had turned into a currency loose enough to tip with.

Then she wrote:

Ben,

Per our conversation, when I prepare the workshop extension of the robustness paper this spring, I will include you as an author.

Leela

It was concise, disgusting, and entirely legible. Ben leaned in just enough to verify the line. Omar said nothing. The green status marker stayed green.

She hit send.

In the outbox, the message to Ben sat directly above the automatic receipt confirming that Omar Elmasry's updated funding materials had moved from INCOMPLETE to SUBMITTED. On the screen the two emails looked as if they had been generated by the same office.
state_deltas_required: June, Leela, and Omar try to assemble a grievance file strong enough to survive Haviland's preference for containment.
They submit a weakened grievance, and June sees that Leela's instinct for self-protection remains stronger than her declared solidarity.
